regions: Nationwide — hospitals and training schools concentrated in urban centers
Rural areas served by visiting/public health nurses after 1900
Military nursing: Army camps, naval hospitals, overseas theaters (WWI, WWII)
Southern states: separate training programs and hospitals for Black nurses

what_they_did.daily_work: Patient care encompassed wound dressing, medication administration, temperature and pulse monitoring, bathing and feeding patients, maintaining ward cleanliness, and detailed record-keeping. Before the 1940s, the majority of nursing was private duty — a nurse hired by a family to care for a sick or injured member in the patient's home, working alone with minimal supervision. Hospital nursing became dominant after 1930, involving shift work on wards under physician direction. Public health nurses made home visits in immigrant and low-income neighborhoods, providing well-baby care, tuberculosis screening, and health education.
what_they_did.seasonal_cycle: Nursing work was year-round, though demand for private duty nurses spiked during epidemic seasons — influenza in winter, typhoid and polio in summer. Public health nursing campaigns often intensified in fall (school health inspections) and spring (vaccination drives).
tools_and_equipment: Nursing bag (for visiting nurses — contained basic supplies for home visits)
what_they_did.typical_hours: Student nurses worked 12-hour shifts, 6-7 days a week, as part of their training. Private duty nurses were on call around the clock — a case might require 20 consecutive hours of attendance. Hospital nurses worked 8-12 hour shifts after the 1930s, when labor reforms began to take effect.

economics.typical_arrangement: Student nurses received no wages — they provided free labor to the training hospital in exchange for education, room, and board. The training school system was, in practice, a source of cheap labor for hospitals. Private duty nurses charged a daily fee paid by the patient's family. Hospital staff nurses earned a monthly salary, often with room and board included.
economics.annual_income_range: Private duty nurses 1900s-1910s: $10-$15/week when employed, but employment was irregular — many weeks brought no cases. Hospital nurses 1920s-1930s: $50-$100/month with room and board. By 1945-1950: $150-$250/month. Public health nurses were typically the best-compensated, earning $75-$125/month by the 1920s.
economics.comparison: A trained nurse in 1910 earned roughly as much as a skilled male factory worker when employed, but private duty work was erratic and many nurses experienced long stretches without income. The prestige of the profession was not matched by economic security until hospital employment became the norm.
economics.debt_cycle: Student nurses accumulated no monetary debt but invested 2-3 years of unpaid labor. Some hospitals charged fees for breakage, books, and uniforms that were deducted from the nominal stipends some schools offered. The real cost was opportunity — years of unpaid work during which the student could have been earning wages elsewhere.

hazards: Infectious disease exposure (tuberculosis, influenza, typhoid, polio)
Physical strain from lifting and moving patients
Exhaustion from extended shifts and on-call duty
Exposure to chemical disinfectants (carbolic acid, mercury compounds)
working_conditions.living_conditions: Student and hospital nurses typically lived in nursing residences ('nurses' homes') attached to the hospital — dormitory-style rooms with strict curfews, dress codes, and behavioral rules. Private duty nurses lived in their own lodgings when between cases and in the patient's home during a case. The nursing residence enforced a quasi-military discipline that persisted well into the 1950s.
working_conditions.family_involvement: Nursing was overwhelmingly a single woman's occupation until the mid-20th century. Most hospitals required nurses to resign upon marriage. Married nurses who continued to work were the exception, not the rule, until severe wartime labor shortages forced hospitals to hire married women during WWII. Male nurses existed but were rare and typically worked in psychiatric institutions or as military corpsmen.
working_conditions.autonomy: Private duty nurses had considerable autonomy — alone with a patient for hours, they made continuous clinical judgments. Hospital nurses operated under strict physician authority and hierarchical supervision (superintendent of nurses, head nurse, floor nurse). Public health nurses, working alone in the community, exercised the greatest professional independence.

push_factors: Marriage bars forced resignation from hospital positions
Physical and emotional exhaustion from the demands of patient care
Low pay relative to the skill and responsibility required
Racial exclusion blocked career advancement for Black nurses
Irregular employment plagued private duty nurses, especially during the Depression
why_they_left.what_replaced_them: The profession did not disappear — it transformed. Private duty nursing gave way to hospital-based employment. Practical nursing (LPN) programs created a lower tier of nursing after 1940. The nursing shortage became chronic by the 1950s as demand outstripped the supply of trained graduates.
why_they_left.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} trained as a nurse in {county} around {year}, she likely entered the hospital as a student and worked for free while learning — and the education she received would have equipped her to handle medical emergencies that most physicians of the previous generation could not.

records_generated.record_type: Nursing school enrollment and graduation records
records_generated.what_it_shows: Student name, dates of enrollment and graduation, affiliated hospital, sometimes hometown and next of kin
records_generated.where_to_find: Hospital archives (many still held by the successor institution), university archives where training schools were affiliated, state nursing associations
records_generated.research_tip: Many nursing school records survive because hospitals maintained them for credentialing purposes. If you know the hospital, contact its archive or the local historical society. Nursing school yearbooks and class photographs are also common.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Name, date of licensure, training school, license number, renewals
records_generated.where_to_find: State boards of nursing (most states began licensing after 1903 — North Carolina was first). Historical records may be in state archives.
records_generated.research_tip: After 1903, state licensure records are the most reliable way to confirm nursing credentials. Not all states required licensure immediately — some allowed unlicensed practice until the 1930s-1940s.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Name, rank, dates of service, assignments, unit, overseas service, decorations
records_generated.where_to_find: NARA (Army Nurse Corps records in Record Group 112, Navy Nurse Corps in Record Group 52). National Personnel Records Center, St. Louis.
records_generated.research_tip: The Army Nurse Corps was established in 1901, the Navy Nurse Corps in 1908. Military nursing records are among the most complete personnel files for women in this era. WWII-era records may have been affected by the 1973 NPRC fire — request what survives.

records_generated.record_type: Hospital annual reports and employee directories
records_generated.what_it_shows: Names of nursing staff, sometimes with hire dates, positions, and departments
records_generated.where_to_find: Hospital archives, county and city historical societies, medical library special collections
records_generated.research_tip: Large urban hospitals published annual reports that listed their nursing staff. These are most useful for confirming employment at a specific institution in a specific year.

census_clues.what_to_look_for: A woman listed as 'student nurse' living in a hospital dormitory is in training. A 'trained nurse' or 'graduate nurse' boarding in a private home is likely between private duty cases. A nurse listed in a hospital household is either on staff or a student. Note that before 1900, 'nurse' in the census could mean a domestic caretaker with no formal training.
census_clues.red_flags: A woman listed as 'nurse' in one census and 'housewife' in the next likely married and was forced to leave the profession. This was the norm, not the exception, before the 1950s.

cultural_context: The professionalization of nursing from the 1870s through the 1940s represents one of the most significant expansions of women's roles in American public life. Trained nurses occupied a space between the domestic sphere and the male-dominated medical profession — technically subordinate to physicians but performing the actual hands-on care that determined whether patients lived or died. For Black women, nursing was one of the few professional paths available, but segregated training and practice meant constant battles for recognition and equal treatment.

counter_narratives.claim: Nursing was not always a respected profession
counter_narratives.context: Before the Civil War, nursing was associated with domestic servants and, in some public perception, with disreputable women. Charles Dickens's character Sairey Gamp — a drunken, incompetent nurse — reflected widespread attitudes. The professionalization campaign led by Florence Nightingale and American reformers deliberately constructed nursing as a respectable vocation for middle-class women, but the stigma lingered for decades.

counter_narratives.claim: Black nurses were systematically excluded, not simply underrepresented
counter_narratives.context: Most nursing schools refused to admit Black students until well into the 20th century. Black nurses trained at a small number of institutions — Lincoln School for Nurses in New York (1898), Freedmen's Hospital at Howard University, Tuskegee Institute, and a handful of others. The National Association of Colored Graduate Nurses (1908-1951) fought for integration into the American Nurses Association, which did not admit Black members in many state chapters until the 1940s-1950s. The Army Nurse Corps did not accept Black nurses until 1941, and the Navy Nurse Corps not until 1945.

counter_narratives.claim: Military nursing was not a safe, rear-echelon assignment
counter_narratives.context: Over 400 American nurses died during WWI, most from the 1918 influenza pandemic. In WWII, Army nurses were captured at Bataan and Corregidor and spent three years as prisoners of war. Navy nurses served on hospital ships under enemy fire. Sixty-seven Army nurses were captured by the Japanese in the Philippines. Military nursing was dangerous service, not a sheltered support role.

source_references.author: Reverby, Susan M.
source_references.title: Ordered to Care: The Dilemma of American Nursing, 1850-1945
source_references.notes: The foundational academic history of American nursing, essential for understanding the economics and power dynamics of the profession

source_references.author: Hine, Darlene Clark
source_references.title: Black Women in White: Racial Conflict and Cooperation in the Nursing Profession, 1890-1950
source_references.notes: Definitive study of Black nurses' experiences and the fight for professional recognition and integration

source_references.author: D'Antonio, Patricia
source_references.title: American Nursing: A History of Knowledge, Authority, and the Meaning of Work
source_references.notes: Comprehensive history spanning the full arc of nursing professionalization with attention to class, race, and gender

source_references.author: Tomblin, Barbara Brooks
source_references.title: G.I. Nightingales: The Army Nurse Corps in World War II
source_references.notes: Detailed account of military nursing in WWII including combat zone service, POW experiences, and racial integration
